title: Woven rag rug on a rigid heddle loom
subtitle: Strips of worn fabric woven as weft into a sturdy cotton warp
excerpt: A 40 cm × 70 cm rug woven on a rigid heddle loom using torn fabric strips as weft and 8/4 cotton rug warp at 5 ends per inch. The strips are torn from old cotton sheets, T-shirts, or denim into continuous lengths and woven firmly to produce a thick, durable surface. One of the most approachable first rug projects: it uses basic weaving skills and repurposes material that would otherwise be discarded.
sourceNotes:
- Inventive Weaving on a Little Loom (Syne Mitchell): rigid heddle rug warp sett, rag weft preparation
- Jane Patrick, The Weaver's Idea Book: rag strip joining, beating technique for rugs
- General rigid heddle practice: wide sett for thick weft, hemstitching to finish

glossaryTerms.definition: The set of threads held under tension along the length of the loom, through which the weft is interlaced to create woven fabric.
The thread or strip passed back and forth across the warp during weaving; in a rag rug, torn fabric strips replace conventional yarn as weft.
The number of warp ends per inch (epi); a wider sett (fewer ends per inch) leaves more space for a thick weft to pack in and create a weft-faced fabric.
The finished side edge of woven cloth; in rug weaving, maintaining a clean, even selvedge prevents the rug from drawing in.
A finishing stitch worked through the warp and weft at the start and end of a woven piece to prevent unravelling; creates a neat, secure edge without a knot.
A weave structure in which the weft covers the warp completely on the surface; typical of rugs and tapestries where the warp should not show.

content.text: A rag rug on a rigid heddle loom is one of the most satisfying ways to use up worn-out fabric. The
warp
is a strong 8/4 cotton rug warp at 5 ends per inch; the wide
sett
leaves room for the thick
weft
to pack in tightly and produce a
weft-faced
fabric where no warp shows on the surface. Finished size is approximately 40 cm wide × 70 cm long (on the loom); expect 10-15% shrinkage in length after the rug is taken off the loom and washed.
Setup: preparing rag strips and warping

Step 1: Prepare the rag strips. Wash all fabric before tearing. For cotton sheets: cut a 2-3 cm notch along the selvedge and then tear across the width; the tear follows the grain and produces a straight strip. For T-shirt jersey: cut with scissors along the grain into 2-3 cm wide strips (jersey does not tear cleanly). For denim: use scissors to cut 2-3 cm wide strips with the grain; denim is dense and should be cut narrower (1.5-2 cm) to keep the weft from becoming too thick.

Step 2: Join strips end to end. For a flat join: overlap the end of one strip over the start of the next by 3-4 cm and continue weaving; the overlap will be hidden in the weft. For a knotted join: make a small diagonal cut 1 cm from the end of each strip, pass one end through the other, and pull firmly. Do not use bulky knots that will sit proud on the surface.
Step 3: Wind joined strips into balls for easier handling during weaving.

Step 4: Warp the loom. Use a 5 epi (ends per inch) rigid heddle. For a 40 cm wide rug, warp 20 cm on each side of centre, giving approximately 80 warp ends (8 per 2 cm × 20 cm). Thread the loom following the manufacturer's instructions for the rigid heddle; use a 5-dent heddle if available, or space threads to achieve 5 epi in a wider heddle. Wind the warp to 90 cm in length to allow for take-up, loom waste, and fringe.
Weaving
Step 5: Weave a header. Before beginning with rag strips, weave 2-3 cm with scrap yarn in plain weave. This spreads the warp evenly and gives a firm edge against which to begin the rag weft.
Step 6: Begin the rag weft. Open the first shed (raise the heddle). Pass the rag strip through the shed, leaving a 5 cm tail at the start. Do not pull the strip tight at the
selvedge
: leave a small arc of extra weft across the width so that when you beat it down, the strip has enough length to pass over and under every warp end without pulling in the edges.

Step 7: Beat firmly. Press the weft down with the rigid heddle on each pick. Rug weaving requires a firm beat; the strip should pack solidly against the previous row and the warp should not be visible from the front.

Step 8: Change sheds and continue. Alternate the shed position with each pass. Check that the selvedge edges are even: the rug should not narrow as it grows. If drawing in occurs, open the shed, lay the new weft in a looser arc, and re-beat.
Step 9: Colour changes. To change colour or fabric type, end the current strip at a selvedge and begin the new strip at the same selvedge, overlapping by one pick so the end is locked in.
Finishing
Step 10: Weave a closing header of 2-3 cm in scrap yarn to mirror the opening header.
Step 11: Work
hemstitch

along both ends using a blunt darning needle and a length of warp yarn. Group 2-4 warp ends per stitch and work the needle under the bundle and back through the loop to secure the weft rows and prevent unravelling.

Step 12: Cut off the loom, leaving at least 10 cm of warp at each end for fringe. Remove the scrap yarn header. Tie the warp ends in overhand knots in groups of 4-6 to secure the weft rows, then trim the fringe to a consistent length (typically 5-8 cm).

Step 13: Wash and block. Hand-wash the rug in cool water with a small amount of pH-neutral detergent. Press firmly between towels to remove water; do not wring. Lay flat to dry, stretching to planned dimensions and pinning the corners to keep the shape while drying.
Troubleshooter

attrs.problem: Rug narrows toward the centre (drawing in)

content.text: The weft is being pulled too tightly. Lay each strip in a wide upward arc across the shed before beating; this reserves the extra length the strip needs to pass over and under warp ends across the full width without pulling the selvedges inward.

attrs.problem: Warp shows through on the surface

content.text: The strips are too narrow or the beat is too light. Cut strips wider (2.5-3 cm) and beat more firmly with each pick. The strip should compress the previous row clearly downward on each beat.

attrs.problem: Joins between strips are visible as bumps on the surface

content.text: Use the flat overlap join rather than a knotted join. Position joins so they fall in the middle of the rug width, not at the selvedge, and stagger them across rows so no two joins appear in the same area.
